questions.question: Discharge by agreement occurs where:

options.A: One party unilaterally decides to abandon the contract without the other's consent
options.B: The parties mutually agree to release each other from their outstanding contractual obligations
options.C: A court orders the discharge of the contract on grounds of unfairness
options.D: A statutory provision automatically discharges the parties following a change in the law

questions.explanation: Discharge by agreement arises where both parties consent to the termination or modification of their contractual obligations. This can take several forms: bilateral (mutual) release — where both parties still have outstanding obligations and each releases the other (supported by consideration through the exchange of releases); unilateral waiver — where only one party has outstanding obligations and the other agrees to release them; or accord and satisfaction — where a new agreement is substituted for the original. The requirement of consideration is central to analysing these situations.

questions.question: A bilateral discharge agreement — where both parties still have outstanding obligations — is enforceable without any additional formality because:

options.A: The courts give effect to any agreement made in writing between commercial parties
options.B: Each party's promise to release the other from their outstanding obligations provides consideration for the other's reciprocal release — there is an exchange of value
options.C: Once a contract is formed, any subsequent agreement between the parties to modify it is automatically binding
options.D: Bilateral discharge agreements are governed by statute and require no consideration

questions.explanation: A bilateral discharge is straightforwardly enforceable because it is itself a contract — each party's promise to release the other from their outstanding obligations provides consideration for the other's promise to do likewise. The exchange of releases is an exchange of valuable rights — each party surrenders their right to performance by the other. This is distinguished from a unilateral release (waiver) where one party alone has outstanding obligations — the release of that party requires either consideration from them or execution as a deed.

questions.question: Accord and satisfaction refers to the process whereby:

options.A: A court supervises the parties' agreement to modify their contractual obligations
options.B: The parties agree to substitute a new obligation for an existing one (the accord), which is then performed (the satisfaction) — the original obligation is then discharged
options.C: One party accepts the other's partial performance as full satisfaction of the contractual obligation
options.D: A third party guarantor satisfies the contractual obligation on behalf of a defaulting party

questions.explanation: Accord and satisfaction is a two-stage process: (1) accord — the parties agree to a new arrangement to discharge the existing obligation (e.g., accept £500 in settlement of a claim for £1,000); and (2) satisfaction — the new arrangement is performed. Only when both stages are complete is the original obligation discharged. If the accord is agreed but not yet satisfied (performed), the creditor can still sue on the original obligation — the accord alone does not discharge the debt. This is the significance of the 'satisfaction' element: the accord is essentially a conditional compromise.

questions.question: In British Russian Gazette & Trade Outlook Ltd v Associated Newspapers Ltd [1933] (CA), the court confirmed that where there is a binding accord but satisfaction has not yet been performed:

options.A: The original obligation is suspended pending satisfaction
options.B: The original obligation is immediately discharged by the accord itself
options.C: The creditor has the right to sue on the original obligation — the accord is a conditional arrangement that only discharges upon satisfaction
options.D: The creditor must elect between suing on the original obligation and enforcing the accord

questions.explanation: The position is that a bare accord — the agreement to accept something in substitution — does not by itself discharge the original obligation. Until the substituted performance (the satisfaction) is actually rendered, the creditor retains the right to sue on the original obligation. If the debtor refuses to perform the accord, the creditor may sue on the original claim. If, however, the accord is itself a binding contract supported by consideration (e.g., a promise to pay the lower sum), the creditor may be bound not to sue on the original pending performance of the accord.

questions.question: Waiver in contract law refers to the voluntary relinquishment of a known right. A common law waiver (as distinct from promissory estoppel) requires:

options.A: A written agreement signed by the party waiving the right
options.B: Consideration — a unilateral promise to forego a right is not binding at common law without something given in return
options.C: Only a clear expression of intent to waive the right, whether or not consideration is provided
options.D: Registration with the relevant court to be legally effective

questions.explanation: At common law, a waiver — a promise to forego the exercise of a contractual right — requires consideration to be binding. A bare promise to forgive a debt or to overlook a breach is not enforceable at common law without consideration in return. This is why the common law doctrine of waiver and the equitable doctrine of promissory estoppel both developed alongside the consideration requirement. Where one party provides no consideration for the other's promise to waive a right, reliance on the doctrine of promissory estoppel may be necessary.

questions.question: An agreement by which parties agree to discharge their contract and replace it with a new agreement is known as:
questions.explanation: Novation is the process by which an existing contract is replaced by a new one — the original contract is discharged and a new contract comes into being. Novation most commonly occurs where a new party is substituted for one of the original parties (e.g., a company is substituted as debtor for an individual). Unlike assignment (which can transfer benefits without the other party's consent), novation requires the agreement of all parties — including any new party — because the original contract is being extinguished and replaced. Novation requires consideration (usually the promise of the new party).

questions.question: A contractual variation — where the parties agree to modify the terms of an existing contract — is only binding if:

options.A: It is in writing and signed by both parties
options.B: It is supported by consideration from the party seeking the variation
options.C: It satisfies the requirements of a binding contract — offer, acceptance, consideration (unless by deed), and intention to create legal relations
options.D: It is approved by a court or regulatory authority

questions.explanation: A contractual variation is itself a contract and must satisfy all the requirements of a valid contract: offer, acceptance, consideration (unless executed as a deed), and intention to create legal relations. The consideration requirement can cause difficulties — particularly where only one party is seeking the variation and offers no new benefit to the other. The doctrine in Williams v Roffey Bros [1991] (practical benefit as consideration) has made it easier to find consideration for variations, but the core principle remains: a bare agreement to modify an existing contract for no return is not binding at common law.

questions.question: A 'no oral modification' (NOM) clause in a commercial contract provides that any variation must be in writing. What is the current English law position on the effect of such clauses?

options.A: Such clauses are always overridden by subsequent oral agreements between the parties
options.B: Such clauses are valid and enforceable — a purported oral variation of a contract containing a NOM clause is ineffective at law, though estoppel may operate in exceptional circumstances
options.C: Such clauses are only valid in consumer contracts regulated by the Consumer Rights Act 2022
options.D: Such clauses are void as a matter of public policy because they unduly restrict the parties' freedom to modify their arrangement

questions.explanation: In Rock Advertising Ltd v MWB Business Exchange Centres Ltd [2018] (UK SC), the Supreme Court upheld the validity and enforceability of NOM clauses. Lord Sumption held that such clauses serve legitimate purposes (certainty, protection against informal variations) and should be given effect. A purported oral variation of a contract subject to a NOM clause is ineffective — the parties have agreed on a formality requirement for variations. However, the Court left open the possibility that estoppel might in exceptional circumstances prevent a party from relying on the NOM clause. Note: the Irish position has not been authoritatively determined at the highest level.

questions.question: Where a creditor agrees to accept a lesser sum in full satisfaction of a debt, this is known as:

options.B: Accord and satisfaction (if consideration is provided) or a unilateral release (if by deed)
options.D: Rescission of the original loan contract

questions.explanation: An agreement to accept a lesser sum in satisfaction of a debt can operate as accord and satisfaction if the debtor provides some fresh consideration alongside payment of the lesser sum (e.g., pays early, pays in different form, or provides some additional benefit). Without consideration, the creditor's promise to accept less is not binding under the rule in Foakes v Beer [1884]. A unilateral release executed as a deed (by deed poll) can discharge the debt without consideration. Promissory estoppel may prevent the creditor from suing for the balance in certain circumstances (High Trees [1947]) but does not itself provide a binding discharge.

questions.question: Rescission by agreement (contrasted with rescission for vitiating factors such as misrepresentation) occurs where:

options.A: A court declares a contract void for a defect in its formation
options.B: The parties mutually agree to abandon the contract and be released from future obligations, either restoring the original position or leaving matters as they are
options.C: One party elects to terminate for breach and the other party consents
options.D: A party invokes a contractual right to terminate at will

questions.explanation: Rescission by agreement is a form of discharge by agreement — the parties mutually agree to abandon the contract. It is distinct from rescission for a vitiating factor (which sets the contract aside ab initio). Rescission by agreement may be either: (1) total — the parties abandon the contract and are returned to their pre-contractual positions; or (2) partial — the parties discharge some obligations but retain others. The agreement to rescind is itself a contract requiring consideration (normally the mutual release of outstanding obligations). Where the contract has been fully performed by one party, rescission may not be possible without fresh consideration.

questions.question: In the context of a compromise agreement (settling a dispute by agreement), the consideration for the promise to pay a sum in settlement is:

options.A: The settlement amount itself — the payment provides the consideration
options.B: The forbearance from suing on the original claim — giving up the right to litigate is valuable consideration
options.C: There is no consideration required for compromise agreements — they are binding as a special class of contract
options.D: The mutual recognition by both parties that their dispute has some merit

questions.explanation: The consideration in a compromise (settlement) agreement is the forbearance from suing on the original claim. Each party gives up their right to pursue or defend the claim in exchange for the other's concession. Giving up a legal right — including the right to litigate — is good consideration even where the claim relinquished might ultimately prove unsuccessful, provided the claimant honestly believed in the claim and it was not frivolous: Cook v Wright [1861]. The compromise agreement is therefore a binding contract once the elements of offer, acceptance, consideration, and intention to create legal relations are met.

questions.question: A 'subject to contract' agreement to compromise a dispute is:

options.A: Immediately binding because the parties have agreed the essential terms
options.B: Not binding until a formal settlement agreement is executed, because the parties have signified they do not intend to be bound until formal documents are exchanged
options.C: Binding in equity even if not in writing
options.D: Binding only if both parties are represented by solicitors

questions.explanation: 'Subject to contract' negotiations indicate that the parties do not intend to be bound until formal written settlement agreements are signed. This is the same principle that applies to 'subject to contract' in property transactions: Courtney v McCarthy [2007] (SC Ireland) confirmed the importance of this phrase in negating binding intention until contract formalities are completed. A settlement agreement marked 'subject to contract' is not binding — either party may withdraw before formal agreements are executed, even if the other has incurred expense in reliance on the negotiations.

questions.question: Under Irish procedural rules, where parties to litigation reach a settlement agreement in the form of a 'consent order' or 'consent judgment,' what is the effect?

options.A: The settlement has no binding effect until the court has reviewed and approved the substantive terms
options.B: The settlement is embodied in a court order — it has the force of a court judgment and can be enforced through court processes without the need to bring a fresh action
options.C: The settlement creates a new contract between the parties but cannot be enforced as a court order
options.D: The consent order discharges the original claim but the parties retain all other rights under the original contract

questions.explanation: A consent order or consent judgment is a court order made with the agreement of the parties that embodies the terms of a settlement. Because it is a court order, it has the force of a judgment — breach can be enforced by the contempt of court procedure, examination of judgment debtor, and other enforcement mechanisms. The consent order merges the settlement agreement into the court's process, which provides stronger enforcement than a purely contractual settlement (which would require a fresh action for breach of the settlement agreement).

questions.question: A contractual term providing for automatic termination in the event of a specified occurrence (a 'termination event clause') operates as:

options.A: An example of discharge by breach
options.B: A form of agreed discharge — the parties have agreed in advance the circumstances in which the contract will automatically be discharged
options.C: An option to terminate exercisable at the innocent party's discretion
options.D: A penalty clause enforceable regardless of the actual loss suffered

questions.explanation: A termination event clause or automatic termination clause is a contractual mechanism by which the parties agree in advance that the contract will be discharged automatically upon the occurrence of a specified event (e.g., insolvency, regulatory change, change of control). This is a form of agreed discharge — the parties have contractually provided for the circumstances in which their obligations will end. It is distinct from breach (which requires a breach by one party and an election by the other) and from frustration (which is an external event not provided for by the contract).

questions.question: An option clause giving one party the right to terminate the contract at will upon giving notice is:

options.A: Void as an illusory obligation — the contract is unenforceable for lack of mutuality
options.B: A valid contractual right to unilateral discharge — exercise of the right discharges both parties' future obligations
options.C: Valid only if the other party has an equivalent right to terminate
options.D: A form of liquidated damages clause entitling the terminating party to compensation

questions.explanation: An option clause giving one party the right to terminate at will (sometimes called a 'break clause') is a valid contractual right. Exercise of the right in accordance with the contract — by giving the required notice — discharges both parties from future obligations under the contract. There is no requirement for mutuality of termination rights. Break clauses are common in commercial leases, service agreements, and employment contracts. The party exercising the break clause is not liable for breach — they are exercising a contractual right and the other party has no claim for the loss of anticipated future benefits.

questions.question: Where one party has fully performed their obligations under a contract and the other party proposes to discharge the remaining (unpaid) obligation by agreement, what is required to make the discharge binding?

options.A: The performed party's express consent to the arrangement
options.B: The performed party must receive some fresh consideration in return for agreeing to release the other from the unpaid obligation, unless the release is by deed
options.C: Both parties must sign a formal deed of release for the discharge to be effective
options.D: The discharge is valid because there is no longer any consideration requirement once one party has fully performed

questions.explanation: Where one party has fully performed their obligations, the doctrine of bilateral discharge does not apply — there is nothing left to exchange between the parties as consideration for a release. To discharge the other party's unpaid obligation, the fully-performed party must either: (1) receive some fresh consideration for agreeing to release (e.g., payment of a lesser sum early, some new benefit); (2) execute the release by deed (which requires no consideration under seal); or (3) rely on promissory estoppel in appropriate cases. A bare oral promise to forgive a debt without consideration is not binding at common law: Foakes v Beer [1884].

questions.question: A 'standstill agreement' in the context of commercial negotiations is an agreement by which:

options.A: The parties agree to maintain the existing contractual position and suspend performance obligations for a specified period
options.B: The parties agree not to sue on an existing claim for a specified period while they attempt to resolve their dispute
options.C: A creditor agrees not to enforce a judgment debt while the debtor arranges refinancing
options.D: Both A and B are correct descriptions of contexts in which standstill agreements are used

questions.explanation: Standstill agreements are contractual arrangements in which the parties agree to maintain the status quo for a defined period. They are used in at least two distinct contexts: (1) in ongoing contractual performance — to pause obligations while a dispute or renegotiation is resolved; and (2) in litigation — to agree not to issue or continue proceedings for a period (commonly to toll limitation periods while settlement negotiations continue). In both cases, the standstill is itself a contract requiring consideration, typically the mutual forbearance from taking steps each party might otherwise take.

questions.question: In the Irish case of Revenue Commissioners v Moroney [1972] (SC Ireland), which concerned the effect of an agreement to compromise on subsequent litigation, the Supreme Court confirmed:

options.A: A compromise agreement can always be set aside if one party later discovers additional facts
options.B: A valid compromise agreement, once concluded with consideration, is binding on the parties and bars fresh litigation on the original dispute — the compromise itself becomes the parties' contractual relationship
options.C: A compromise of a tax dispute cannot be binding on the Revenue without statutory authority
options.D: A compromise reached in correspondence is not binding until embodied in a formal court order

questions.explanation: Revenue Commissioners v Moroney [1972] confirmed the binding effect of a properly concluded compromise agreement in Irish law. A valid compromise — supported by consideration (typically mutual forbearance and concession) — is a binding contract that bars fresh litigation on the original claim. The compromise replaces the original dispute with a new contractual obligation. This reflects the fundamental principle that finality in dispute resolution is a public interest: parties should not be able to reopen settled matters simply because they believe, with hindsight, that they agreed to an unfavourable outcome.
